title: Grassland Habitat
description: A herd of antelope moves slowly through the tall grass. Suddenly a cheetah leaps from its hiding place, and the animals zig and zag across the savanna. They make it to a small grove of scrubby trees, but they can’t let their guard down yet. It’s the end of the rainy season and the antelopes—like many animals on the grasslands—must leave their protected place and migrate across open grasslands to find water.
author: Avery Hurt

title: China
description: Stretching 3,100 miles (5,000 kilometers) from east to west and 3,400 miles (5,500 kilometers) from north to south, China is a large country with widely varying landscapes.

title: Freshwater Habitat
description: An alligator dozes on a log. A heron soars overhead and lands in the reeds a safe distance away. Sploop! A frog jumps into the water while insects buzz and chirp. This freshwater habitat is a busy place!
author: Avery Hurt

title: Save animals!
description: You can help these critters. Find out how.
author: Allyson Shaw

title: 10 tips to reduce your plastic use
description: Want to save the Earth and its oceans? Eat ice cream in a cone! Seriously, single-use plastic items like ice cream spoons account for more than 40 percent of plastic waste, and each year about 8.8 million tons of plastic trash flows into the ocean. And that waste endangers wildlife. But solving the plastic problem can be as easy as getting your sweet treat in a cone. Check out our top 10 quick tips to reduce your single-use plastic pollution today!
author: Allyson Shaw

title: 13 ways to save the Earth from climate change
description: Easy ways to help
author: Allyson Shaw

title: 13 ways to save the Earth from declining biodiversity
description: See how you can make a difference.
author: Allyson Shaw

title: 13 ways to save the Earth from pollution
description: You might use plastic water bottles, yogurt cups, and straws for just a day, but they can remain in the environment for years. And that pollution can harm habitats and the animals that live there. Cut down Earth’s trash with these tips.
author: Allyson Shaw

title: 13 ways to save the Earth from habitat destruction
description: Your actions make a difference!
author: Allyson Shaw

title: Lunch Bag
description: Help keep the Earth healthy by ditching single-use plastic items. Make a reusable bag to carry your lunch so you don't have to buy plastic bags and boxes, which take up landfill space.
author: Ella Schwartz

title: Paper Straws
description: Help keep the Earth healthy by ditching single-use plastic items. You can make a paper straw to use instead of a plastic one, which is one of the top items found at beach cleanups and can hurt ocean animals that mistake them for food.
author: Allyson Shaw

title: Cookies in a Jar
description: Help keep the Earth healthy by ditching single-use plastic items. Make gifts to share with your friends, family, and teachers instead of buying something from the store, which might come wrapped in plastic. Want more holiday gift ideas? Download a guide to plastic-free gift giving!
author: Ella Schwartz

title: Plastic Pollution
description: What's the problem?
author: Allyson Shaw

title: Ice Pops
description: Help keep the Earth healthy by ditching single-use plastic items. Make your own ice pops so you can skip the plastic-wrapped store-bought version. Left in outdoor trash cans, these wrappers can easily be blown into the environment, where animals might mistake them for food.
author: Ella Schwartz

title: Cutlery holder
description: Help keep the Earth healthy by ditching single-use plastic items. Make a cutlery holder to carry reusable forks, spoons, and knives so you can skip the plastic utensils. Why? Litter from take-out meals is a common type of trash found during beach cleanups.
author: Ella Schwartz

title: Yummy Yogurt
description: Help keep the Earth healthy by ditching single-use plastic items. Make your own yogurt instead of buying it in plastic cups at the store. These plastic containers can be dangerous to wild animals like raccoons and squirrels when they get their heads stuck in them.
author: Ella Schwartz

title: Paper Beads
description: Help keep the Earth healthy by ditching single-use plastic items. Make art beads out of old paper products instead of using plastic beads, which animals might mistake for food.
author: Ella Schwartz

title: Pom-Pom Puffs
description: Help keep the Earth healthy by ditching single-use plastic items. This month, plan your next party with paper pom-pom decorations instead of balloons. Why? Balloons released into the air or left outside can end up in the ocean, where they might entangle animals or be mistaken for food.
author: Ella Schwartz

title: Granola Bar Goodies
description: Help keep the Earth healthy by ditching single-use plastic items. Make your own granola bars instead of buying plastic-wrapped snacks. Plastic food wrappers are one of the most commonly found items at beach cleanups. Want to keep snacking? Get your guide to plastic-free treats.
author: Ella Schwartz

title: How animals are affected by plastic
description: Find out how pollution can hurt animals and what you can do to help.

title: Desert Habitat
description: Deserts are the driest places on Earth—they get fewer than 10 inches (25 centimeters) of rain a year. Some deserts may get a lot of rain all at once. Then it might not rain again for months—or even years!
author: Avery Hurt

title: Rainforest Habitat
description: A beam of sunlight makes its way through the leaves and lights up the brightly colored wings of a macaw. An enormous butterfly flits from plant to plant. Monkeys shout and chatter as they swing from tree to tree looking for breakfast. The rainforest is waking up.
author: Avery Hurt

title: Temperate Forest Habitat
description: Dry leaves crunch under your feet as you walk through the woods. The air is crisp and cool. The sun shines through the orange, red, and gold leaves. It’s autumn and the forest is popping with color.
author: Avery Hurt

title: Mountain Habitat
description: It can be a long climb to the top of a mountain, but once you’re there, you can see for miles. It’s like you’re standing on top of the world!
author: Avery Hurt

title: Polar Habitat
description: It’s frigid and you’re surrounded by ice. You wonder how any animal could survive in this extreme cold. Then a polar bear and her cubs lumber by, and in the ocean behind them a whale erupts from the water.
author: Avery Hurt
